title: Bap
subtitle: Soft Scottish morning roll
excerpt: A bap is a large, soft, flour-dusted roll with a tender crumb and an almost imperceptible crust. Traditionally a Scottish breakfast roll, it suits bacon, sausage, or a fried egg. The secret is a small amount of fat in the dough and a light dusting of flour on top to prevent a crust forming during baking.
sourceNotes: Baps are a staple of Scottish and Irish baking. The floury morning roll appears in nineteenth-century Scottish household texts and cookery books. The form (soft, pale, flour-dusted) is documented in F. Marian McNeill, The Scots Kitchen (1929), as the standard morning roll of Lowland Scotland.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze when cool. Defrost at room temperature or split and toast.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven; lower than for a crusty roll, to keep the bap pale and soft
baking.steamMethod: none; the goal is a soft crust

content.text: Baps are large, soft, flour-dusted rolls with almost no crust. The dough is lightly enriched with a little lard or butter, baked at a lower temperature than a crusty roll, and dusted generously with flour before baking. That flour coating prevents a skin forming and keeps the surface pale and pillowy.

Mix flour, yeast, and salt together. Rub in the lard until the mixture looks like fine breadcrumbs. Add the warm milk and water and bring together into a soft dough. Knead for 8 minutes until smooth. The dough should feel silky and slightly softer than a tin-loaf dough.
Cover and leave to rise for 1 hour until doubled.

Divide the dough into 8 pieces. Using a rolling pin, flatten each piece into an oval, roughly 12 × 8 cm. Place on a floured baking tray. Dust generously with flour. Cover loosely and prove for 30 minutes. Before baking, use a finger to press a dimple into the centre of each bap; this prevents a large air pocket forming underneath the top crust.

Heat the oven to 210°C fan. Bake for 13 to 15 minutes. The baps should be very pale gold, almost white on the sides, and still yielding when pressed. An overdone bap has a brown crust; the correct result is soft all the way through. Cool briefly on a wire rack; baps are best eaten warm.
